note_data.title: Further energetics
keyPoints: Transition elements are found in the d-block and form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals
Scandium and zinc are not transition elements because their ions do not have incomplete d subshells - Sc³⁺ has empty d subshell and Zn²⁺ has full d subshell
The 4s orbital is filled before the 3d orbital, but 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons when forming ions
Group 2 carbonates and nitrates become more thermally stable down the group due to decreasing charge density of cations
Smaller Group 2 ions have greater charge density and polarising effect on anions, requiring less heat for thermal decomposition
Group 2 hydroxides become more soluble down the group while sulfates become less soluble down the group
For hydroxides, lattice enthalpy falls faster than enthalpy change of hydration, making solution more exothermic down the group
For sulfates, lattice enthalpy falls slower than hydration enthalpy, making solution more endothermic down the group
The more exothermic the enthalpy of solution, the more soluble a compound is

definitions.definition: Elements in the d-block that form one or more stable ions with incomplete d orbitals
The amount of charge packed into a given volume - smaller ions have greater charge density
The energy required to break up an ionic compound into gaseous ions
The amount of energy released as ions bond to water molecules

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all working steps clearly and include units in your final answer
For Born-Haber cycles, draw arrows showing direction and label all enthalpy changes

modelAnswer.question: Calculate the lattice energy of sodium chloride using the following data: ΔHf°[NaCl(s)] = -411 kJ mol⁻¹, ΔHat°[Na(s)] = +107 kJ mol⁻¹, ΔHat°[Cl₂(g)] = +122 kJ mol⁻¹, ΔHie°[Na(g)] = +496 kJ mol⁻¹, ΔHea°[Cl(g)] = -349 kJ mol⁻¹
modelAnswer.answer: Using Born-Haber cycle and Hess's law: ΔHlatt = ΔHat°[Na] + ½ΔHat°[Cl₂] + ΔHie°[Na] + ΔHea°[Cl] - ΔHf°[NaCl]. ΔHlatt = (+107) + (½ × 122) + (+496) + (-349) - (-411) = +107 + 61 + 496 - 349 + 411. ΔHlatt = +726 kJ mol⁻¹
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct application of Hess's law equation
Mark 2: Correct substitution of values with signs
Mark 3: Correct final answer with units

commonMistakes: Thinking scandium and zinc are transition elements when they are not due to their electron configurations
Confusing the order of electron removal - 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons despite 4s filling first
Mixing up the solubility trends - hydroxides become MORE soluble down Group 2 while sulfates become LESS soluble
Not understanding that thermal stability increases down Group 2 due to decreasing polarising power of cations
examinerTips: Remember that transition elements must have incomplete d orbitals in their ions, not just their atoms
For Group 2 solubility trends, relate back to lattice enthalpy and hydration enthalpy changes to explain the underlying thermodynamics
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Born-Haber cycle energy level diagram showing gaseous ions at the top, with arrows indicating atomisation, ionisation, electron affinity, formation enthalpy and lattice energy changes
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate the enthalpy change of combustion using bond energies and explain why the experimental value differs from the calculated value
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — students had to draw and label an energy level diagram for an exothermic reaction and calculate enthalpy change from formation data
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on identifying the correct definition of enthalpy of atomisation
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — students needed to explain why enthalpy of combustion values from calorimetry are less negative than data book values

workedExample.question: Calculate the enthalpy change for the reaction: C₂H₄ + H₂ → C₂H₆ using the bond energies: C=C: 612 kJ/mol, C-C: 348 kJ/mol, C-H: 412 kJ/mol, H-H: 436 kJ/mol [4 marks]
steps: Step 1: Draw structural formulas and identify bonds broken: 1 × C=C bond (612 kJ/mol), 4 × C-H bonds (4 × 412 = 1648 kJ/mol), 1 × H-H bond (436 kJ/mol)
Step 2: Calculate total energy required to break bonds: 612 + 1648 + 436 = 2696 kJ/mol
Step 3: Identify bonds formed in C₂H₆: 1 × C-C bond (348 kJ/mol), 6 × C-H bonds (6 × 412 = 2472 kJ/mol)
Step 4: Calculate total energy released when bonds form: 348 + 2472 = 2820 kJ/mol
Step 5: Apply formula ΔH = Energy required to break bonds - Energy released when bonds form = 2696 - 2820 = -124 kJ/mol
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct bonds broken, 1 mark for correct bonds formed, 1 mark for correct calculation method, 1 mark for final answer with correct sign

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused enthalpy of formation with enthalpy of combustion' — always check whether the question asks for formation (from elements) or combustion (reaction with oxygen)

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to include the correct sign for enthalpy changes' — always state whether ΔH is positive (endothermic) or negative (exothermic) and include units kJ/mol
Examiner Report 2023: 'Bond energy calculations were often incorrect because students miscounted bonds' — always draw structural formulas clearly showing all bonds before attempting calculations
note_data.keyDiagram: Energy level diagram showing reactants and products on y-axis (energy) vs reaction progress on x-axis. Must include: horizontal lines for reactants (higher) and products (lower) for exothermic reactions, vertical arrow showing ΔH (downward for exothermic, upward for endothermic), activation energy curve showing energy barrier, clear labels for 'Reactants', 'Products', 'ΔH', and 'Activation Energy'. The diagram should show that ΔH is measured from reactant level to product level.
note_data.quickRecap: Transition elements form stable ions with incomplete d orbitals, excluding scandium and zinc. Group 2 compounds show clear trends: thermal stability of carbonates/nitrates increases down the group due to decreasing charge density. Solubility trends are opposite for hydroxides (more soluble down group) and sulfates (less soluble down group) due to different rates of change in lattice enthalpy versus hydration enthalpy.
